message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Dental settings have been considered high risk setting s for COVID-19. A Dental Public Health Team in South East Scotland have worked to risk assess the situation timeously to break chains of transmission.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Aim</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To present routine data produced from a contact tracing service for COVID-19 cases in the dental setting with a focus on transmission.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Observational retrospective analysis of a routine data set of COVID-19 cases associated with a dental setting reported via the national contact tracing system for two health board areas in the east of Scotland.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 cases were confirmed by PCR testing. Descriptive statistics are used to summarise the data collected over a 13-month period (Oct 2020-Dec 2021). A narrative presents themes identified during contact tracing that led to transmission within a dental setting and includes a case study.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A total of 811 incidents are included. No evidence of staff to patient transmission or vice versa was found in this study. Staff to staff transmission occurred in non-clinical areas contributing to 33% of total staff cases.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Transmission of COVID-19 in a dental setting in the context of this study appears to be confined to non-clinical areas. Future pandemic plans should include tools to aid with implementation of guidance in non-clinical areas.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>In brief points</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Outbreaks of COVID-19 in a dental setting appear to be confined to the non-clinical areas of dental practices.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>We have found no evidence of staff to patient transmission or vice versa using our contact tracing methods.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Future pandemic preparedness would benefit from including current quality improvement tools to aid with implementation of new standard operating procedures and other regularly changing guidance.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Surveillance of COVID-19 cases associated with dental settings using routine health data from the East of Scotland with a description of efforts to break chains of transmission from October 2020 to December 2021

reference.unstructured: Public Health England. Public Health England: COVID-19 Background Information. 2020 8/5/20.

Scottish Dental Clinical Effectiveness Programme. Management of Acute Dental Problems During COVID-19 Pandemic 2020 [Available from: https://www.sdcep.org.uk/published-guidance/acute-dental-problems-covid-19/.
Chief Dental Officer Scotland. Remobilisation of NHS Dental Services in Scotland. 2020.
NHS Scotland. Standard Operating Procedures for Dental Teams in Scotland. 2020.

reference.article-title: Risk of SARS-CoV-2 transmission following exposure during dental treatment - A national cohort study
[The REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely-collected health Data (RECORD) statement]
